title: Galatians
author: Paul, the apostle
date: ~AD 48–49 (early date, before the Jerusalem Council) or ~AD 53–55 (later date)
setting: Written to the churches of Galatia (central Asia Minor) who were being influenced by 'Judaizers' — teachers requiring Gentile believers to be circumcised and observe the Mosaic law as a condition of full standing before God.
purpose: To defend the gospel of justification by faith alone against a legalistic perversion that added law-keeping to faith. Paul's tone is urgent and at times sharp — he opens without his usual thanksgiving and calls the gospel-distorters 'accursed' (1:8–9). He grounds his argument in his own apostolic call, the OT example of Abraham, and the freedom that Christ has purchased.
themes: Justification by faith alone — not by law
Freedom in Christ from the law's curse
The gospel cannot be added to
Abraham as the model of faith
Law as tutor pointing to Christ

outline.label: Justification by Faith — the Case from Scripture

before.label: Paul's first missionary journey plants churches in Galatia
Peter withdraws from Gentiles at Antioch — the incident Paul describes
Jewish-Christian teachers demand Gentiles be circumcised

after.label: Jerusalem Council — the gospel for the Gentiles formally affirmed
Romans — the fullest development of justification by faith
Ephesians — the new humanity of Jew and Gentile in Christ

key_people.name: Paul
key_people.role: Author and founder of the Galatian churches who writes with unusual emotional intensity ('I am astonished', 1:6; 'O foolish Galatians!', 3:1) because the gospel itself is at stake

key_people.name: The Agitators
key_people.role: Unnamed Jewish-Christian teachers who have persuaded the Galatians to adopt circumcision and Torah observance as necessary for full membership in God's people — Paul's theological opponents throughout

key_people.name: Peter (Cephas)
key_people.role: Rebuked publicly by Paul at Antioch (2:11-14) for withdrawing from table fellowship with Gentile Christians under pressure from James's people — the incident that precipitates the letter's theological argument

context: Galatians was written c. AD 48-49 (South Galatian theory) or AD 53-55 (North Galatian theory) in response to a crisis: Jewish-Christian teachers had persuaded the Galatian churches that circumcision and Torah observance were necessary for full covenant membership. Paul regards this as a different gospel that perverts the gospel of Christ (1:7). The letter is his most urgent and combative, beginning without a thanksgiving and calling down a curse on those who preach a different gospel.
christ_connection: Galatians argues that the cross accomplishes what the Torah could not: it redeems from the law's curse (3:13), brings the Spirit through faith (3:14), makes all who believe Abraham's descendants and heirs of the promise (3:29), and establishes the new humanity in which ethnic, social, and gender distinctions do not determine covenant standing (3:28). Christ crucified (3:1) is Paul's summary of the entire gospel against every form of works-righteousness. The 'no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me' (2:20) is the personal form of this reality: the Christian life is the life of the risen Christ lived through the believer by faith.

key_verses.note: 'I have been crucified with Christ. It is no longer I who live, but Christ who lives in me' — the most personal expression of union with Christ in Paul's letters
'Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us' — substitutionary atonement in its starkest Pauline form, grounded in Deut 21:23

'There is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus' — the gospel's social implications: covenant standing is equal regardless of ethnicity, status, or gender
'For freedom Christ has set us free; stand firm therefore, and do not submit again to a yoke of slavery' — the practical summary of the entire letter's argument

themes_detail.title: Justification by Faith Against Works of the Law
themes_detail.text: Galatians 2-3 is Paul's most polemical argument for justification by faith: no one is justified by works of the law (2:16); the law cannot give life (3:21); it served as a guardian until Christ came (3:24). The Antioch incident (2:11-14) shows that this is not an abstract doctrine but a practical gospel issue — Peter's behaviour was 'not in step with the truth of the gospel' because it implied that Gentile Christians needed to Judaize to be fully accepted. The theological argument and the social reality are inseparable: a gospel that requires Torah observance for full membership is not a gospel at all.

themes_detail.title: The Promise to Abraham as the Prior and Normative Covenant
themes_detail.text: Galatians 3-4 uses a striking legal argument: a ratified covenant cannot be annulled or modified by a later agreement (3:15). God's covenant with Abraham was a promise received through faith; the Mosaic law, which came 430 years later, cannot retroactively change the terms. The law's function was not to replace the promise but to serve as a guardian until the promised seed (Christ) arrived. Now that Christ has come, the guardian's role is completed: all who are 'in Christ' — regardless of ethnic origin — are Abraham's offspring and heirs of the promise.

themes_detail.title: The Spirit and the Flesh
themes_detail.text: Chapters 5-6 address the ethical corollary of justification by faith: does freedom from the law produce moral license? Paul's answer is the life of the Spirit. The Spirit produces fruit — love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, self-control (5:22-23) — that no list of Torah regulations can produce. The freedom of the gospel is not freedom to indulge the flesh but freedom to serve one another in love (5:13-14). Walking by the Spirit and crucifying the flesh are not human achievement but the life of those who have been united to Christ's death and resurrection.

themes_detail.title: The New Creation and the Unity of the Gospel Community
themes_detail.text: Galatians 6:15 provides the book's ultimate perspective: 'Neither circumcision nor uncircumcision counts for anything, but a new creation.' The gospel does not simply modify the existing order (adding Gentiles to an Israel-shaped community) but inaugurates a new creation in which the old ethnic, social, and gender categories no longer determine covenant standing. The community that lives out Galatians 3:28 — genuinely inclusive across every human boundary — is a sign of the new creation's arrival. Paul's table fellowship with Gentiles that Peter withdrew from was not just a social preference but a sign of the new age.
